plainMeaning: For anyone who bows down and sings the praise of Ajitanātha and Śāntinātha — the two whose faces glow like the full moon — all sin is stilled and good merit keeps growing.
recitation: savvaṁ pasamai pāvaṁ, punnaṁ vaḍḍhai namaṁsamāṇassa; saṁpunna-canda-vayaṇassa, kittaṇaṁ ajia-saṁtissa.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit savva < Skt. sarva; neuter accusative/nominative singular agreeing with pāvaṁ.
Prakrit pasamai < Skt. pra-√śam, praśāmyati 'is extinguished/pacified'; 3rd sg. present.
wordByWord.notes: Same root (√śam) as Śānti — the praise of Śānti yields praśama (quelling); not 'sees' (paśyati).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pāva < Skt. pāpa; neuter, here the grammatical subject of pasamai.
Prakrit punna (doubled -nn-) < Skt. puṇya; neuter nominative singular.
wordByWord.notes: Paired antithetically with pāvaṁ: sin down, merit up.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vaḍḍhai < Skt. √vṛdh, vardhate 'grows'; 3rd sg. present.

wordByWord.gloss: of the one who venerates / for the one bowing in homage
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit present middle participle namaṁsamāṇa < Skt. namasya-māna (denom. namasya- 'pay homage', √nam); genitive singular -ssa.
wordByWord.notes: Genitive of the agent — the fruits accrue 'to/of' this venerator; not a finite 'I bow'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit saṁpunna < Skt. sampūrṇa; first member of the compound saṁpunna-canda-vayaṇa.
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies canda: the unwaning full moon.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit canda < Skt. candra; middle member of the bahuvrīhi 'full-moon-faced'.
Prakrit vayaṇa < Skt. vadana 'face'; genitive singular -ssa, agreeing with ajia-saṁtissa.
wordByWord.notes: saṁpunna-canda-vayaṇassa = 'of the one whose face is the full moon' — a bahuvrīhi epithet of the Jinas.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit kittaṇa < Skt. kīrtana, from √kīrt 'to proclaim, extol'; neuter nominative singular.
wordByWord.notes: The verbal act of praise that is itself the cause of the stated fruits.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ajia < Skt. ajita; first member of the compound ajia-saṁti.
wordByWord.notes: Half of the hymn's signature double name.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit saṁti < Skt. śānti; genitive singular -ssa, governing kittaṇaṁ ('praise of Ajita-Śānti').
wordByWord.notes: Genitive binds the whole epithet-cluster: 'the praise of full-moon-faced Ajita-and-Śānti'.

literalTranslation: All sin subsides, merit increases, of the one venerating — [namely, by] the praise of Ajita-and-Śānti, whose face is the full moon.
idiomaticTranslation: For whoever bows in homage — chanting the eulogy of Ajita and Śānti, those two whose faces shine like the unwaning full moon — every sin is stilled and merit only swells.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This is a compact phala-stuti (statement of fruits) couplet near the hymn's close, and it works by a tight antithesis: sin is the subject of one verb, merit of the other. 'savvaṁ pasamai pāvaṁ' — all sin praśāmyati, is quenched — is balanced by 'punnaṁ vaḍḍhai' — merit vardhate, grows. The two movements (subtraction of pāpa, accretion of puṇya) are the complete arithmetic of devotional benefit. The poet's choice of pasamai (√śam) is not casual: it is the very root of Śānti, so the line enacts what it names — praising Śānti produces śama, pacification. The genitive 'namaṁsamāṇassa' fixes the beneficiary: not the Jinas, who need nothing, but the venerator who bows. The second pāda supplies the object of that homage through a single luminous epithet, 'saṁpunna-canda-vayaṇassa' — 'whose face is the full (unwaning) moon' — here applied jointly to Ajita and Śānti and governed by the genitive 'saṁtissa'. The full-moon face is the hymn's gentleness motif (saumya) crystallised into one image: a countenance that is cool, complete, and flawless. Crucially, the act credited with the fruit is 'kittaṇaṁ' — kīrtana, verbal praise itself — so the verse certifies the worth of the very recitation the devotee is performing as they reach this line.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The couplet rests on the Jain doctrine of two karmic streams — pāpa (demerit) and puṇya (merit) — and speaks in the lay devotional register where bhakti decreases the former and accrues the latter. A practitioner should hold the nuance that, on the higher analysis, even puṇya is auspicious bondage (a golden fetter) rather than liberation itself; the phala-stuti here addresses the reciter's proximate, legitimate aspiration, not the final goal of nirjarā. The verb pra-√śam also names upaśama — the calming of the passions (kaṣāya) — so the 'subsiding' of sin is psychologically, not merely transactionally, framed: praise stills the inner agitation that generates pāpa.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Arriving here, the reciter is told the dividend of the whole stava in one breath — bow, praise, and your sins quiet while your merit grows. Recite it as an act of intention (bhāva): let the word pasamai settle the mind into śama even as it is chanted, treating the kīrtana not as a means to a reward but as the calming itself.

crossReferences.text: Phala-stuti of vv.38–39 (the hymn destroys disease) — same benefit-declaration register
crossReferences.source: Ajitaśānti Stava, Nandiṣeṇasūri (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha Panch Pratikraman ed.)

crossReferences.text: Appended maṅgala 'Upasargāḥ kṣayaṁ yānti…' — sin/affliction-removal motif sealing the recitation

sources.title: Hargovind Das T. Sheth, Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Hindi dictionary), Digital Dictionaries of South Asia
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit glosses and Sanskrit equivalents of pasamai (pra-√śam), vaḍḍhai (√vṛdh), punna (puṇya), pāva (pāpa), kittaṇa (kīrtana).

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams etc.), University of Cologne
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots/derivations: pra-√śam (praśāmyati), √vṛdh (vardhate), √kīrt (kīrtana), namasya-, sampūrṇa, vadana, candra.

sources.title: Jain eLibrary / stotra anthologies (Ajitaśānti Stava recensions and attribution)
sources.consultedFor: Verification of the verse's phala-stuti function within the Ajitaśānti Stava and the ajia-saṁti double-name convention.

notes: Orthography preserved verbatim from the book (પુન્નં doubled -nn-, વઢ્ઢઇ conjunct ḍḍh, સંતિસ્સ genitive). 'namaṁsamāṇassa' is read as a Prakrit present participle in -māṇa (denominative namasya-) in the genitive of the benefiting agent; some recensions/glossing read the line's genitives as a single string ('of the venerator [who utters] the praise of full-moon-faced Ajita-Śānti'), which does not change the sense. 'saṁpunna-canda-vayaṇa' is taken as a bahuvrīhi ('full-moon-faced') jointly qualifying both Jinas; minor pāṭhāntara (variant) spacing/spelling may occur across editions. The pra-√śam / Śānti wordplay is interpretive but well grounded in the shared root.
